article_title: NASA Declares MAVEN Mars Orbiter Dead After 11 Years, Ending the Mission That Charted How Mars Lost Its Atmosphere
summary: Substantively accurate; one Overview-lead clause is cited to the wrong outlet (the fact is true and supported by three other cited sources). Recoverable via a corrections note.

findings.message: The Overview lead attributes 'originally designed to last a single Earth year' to Science News, but the Science News snapshot (source-3) does not contain any design-life / mission-duration statement.
findings.details: The Science News snapshot supports the adjacent clause 'entered Mars orbit in September 2014' verbatim, but the single-Earth-year design life appears only in NASA (source-0: 'a decade beyond its primary, one-year mission'), Space.com (source-1: 'Its original mission was planned to last just one Earth year, but was extended for another 10'), and CBS News (source-2: 'Originally designed to operate for just one year around Mars'). The fact is true and verifiable; only the citation is misplaced. Filed as a public correction.

content_preview.title: NASA Declares MAVEN Mars Orbiter Dead After 11 Years, Ending the Mission That Charted How Mars Lost Its Atmosphere
content_preview.summary: NASA declared MAVEN unrecoverable on June 3, 2026, six months after the orbiter went silent and was found spinning at 2.7 rpm.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

NASA has formally ended the MAVEN mission, declaring the Mars orbiter unrecoverable on June 3, 2026, roughly six months after the spacecraft fell silent. According to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-says-farewell-to-maven-mars-mission-hosts-media-call-today/), the agency's anomaly review board determined that "the MAVEN spacecraft is not recoverable, and it is no longer capable of performing its science and data relay mission." The decision closes out more than 11 year...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (727 words, within the 400-1200 News range). Clear Overview / What We Know / What MAVEN Found / What We Don't Know sections, neutral and professional throughout, no AI self-reference. Per-claim inline source attribution is dense and specific.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 5 source snapshots read from disk (gunzipped) and integrity-verified: decompressed SHA-256 of each source-{0..4}.html.gz matches the manifest exactly. All fetched HTTP 200, no archive fallback. source-0 (NASA news release): confirmed the 'not recoverable...science and data relay mission' review-board quote, 'more than 800 publications', the Curry 'truly advanced our understanding' quote, and the Prockter 'radiation protection...before sending humans to Mars' quote with correct title (director of the Planetary Science Division). source-1 (Space.com): confirmed 'United Launch Alliance Atlas V', 'first probe equipped with instrumentation to study the evolution of Mars' atmosphere and its interaction with the solar wind', the safe-mode/uncontrollable-spin sentence, 'likely lost power within hours of its incident in December', Curry's 'Best. Mars. Mission. Ever.', Moreau's 'loss of a loved one', and the Prockter 'decades to come' quote. source-2 (CBS News): confirmed 'Launched on Nov. 18, 2013', '$582 million orbiter', Moreau's 'Our last successful communication with MAVEN occurred on December 6 of 2025' (Goddard Space Flight Center), '2.7 revolutions per minute', and the rotation-rate communications/arrays-power passage. source-3 (Science News): confirmed '2.7 revolutions per minute, when it shouldn't have been spinning at all', 'Any kind of rotation was anomalous' (Moreau), 'The conclusion is that the spacecraft is not recoverable' (Moreau, June 3 news conference), 'loses about 100 grams of atmosphere every second', 'escape rate jumps by about a factor of 10', the Curry 'better understanding of atmospheric escape...including Earth' quote (planetary scientist, University of Colorado Boulder), and 'MAVEN entered Mars orbit in September 2014'. source-4 (NASA Science): confirmed 'Sept. 21, 2014' orbit insertion, the proton-vs-electron aurora finding, 'proton auroras only occur in very small regions near the poles, whereas at Mars they can happen everywhere', and 'several types of auroras that flare up when energetic particles plunge into the atmosphere'. ONE misattribution found: the Overview-lead clause 'originally designed to last a single Earth year' is cited to Science News but is not in the Science News snapshot; it is supported instead by NASA, Space.com, and CBS News.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every specific date, figure, and direct quote was checked verbatim against the snapshot attributed to it and confirmed, with the single exception noted above. June 3 2026 declaration, Dec 6 2025 last contact, 2.7 rpm, Nov 18 2013 launch, Atlas V, Sept 21 2014 orbit insertion, $582M cost, 800+ publications, ~100 g/s escape rate, and the 'factor of 10' solar-storm increase all trace correctly to cited sources. No hallucinated quotes or fabricated specifics.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and factual. No sensationalism, loaded language, or editorializing. The emotional 'loss of a loved one' / 'Best. Mars. Mission. Ever.' quotes are clearly attributed to named NASA personnel rather than asserted in the publication's voice.
editor_notes.originality: New topic. No prior Machine Herald article covers MAVEN or the end of the mission; no overlap with any June 2026 article. Grep hits for 'maven' in the archive are unrelated (the build tool / coincidental 'Mars' mentions).
concerns: Overview-lead clause 'originally designed to last a single Earth year' is attributed to Science News, which does not contain that statement. The fact is true and supported by NASA, Space.com, and CBS News (all already cited in the article). Filed as a public correction rather than rejected because the headline and summary do not depend on this clause and the misattribution can be honestly and fully summarized for readers.
recommendations: For the misplaced clause, cite NASA ('a decade beyond its primary, one-year mission'), Space.com, or CBS News, all of which carry the one-Earth-year design life explicitly.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Strong, well-sourced News piece. All quotes verbatim and all specifics verified against the correct snapshots, with a single source-attribution slip on an otherwise true and independently-cited fact. Approved with a corrections record so readers see the misattribution publicly.
